title: APIs and Async JavaScript
tagline: Fetch real data from the internet.

accomplishments: Pull real weather data from the internet into your app
Understand how websites get data from servers
Build a weather app that shows live information
Make your apps dynamic with real-world data

do.title: Fetch Real Weather Data
steps: Click 'Get Weather' — see REAL data appear!
Watch the console to see the data flow
do.code:
🌤️ Weather App

Loading weather data...
City not found. Please try again.
Feels Like
Humidity
Wind

do.result: You just fetched REAL weather data from the internet! The data comes from OpenWeatherMap's servers and updates in real-time.
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing weather data for at least 2 different cities.

concepts.name: What is an API?
concepts.definition: API = Application Programming Interface. A URL that returns DATA (usually JSON) instead of a webpage. Websites use them to talk to servers.
concepts.analogy: A restaurant menu. You (the customer) request items from the menu, and the kitchen (the API) prepares and sends back what you ordered.

concepts.name: JSON format
concepts.definition: JavaScript Object Notation — a text format for data that looks almost exactly like JavaScript objects. Keys in double quotes. Almost every API uses JSON.
concepts.analogy: A standardized shipping container — every country uses the same format so anything can be shipped anywhere.

concepts.name: The fetch() function
concepts.definition: fetch(url) makes HTTP requests to get data from URLs. Returns a Promise that resolves when the data arrives. The most important function for working with APIs.
concepts.analogy: Sending a letter — you drop it in the mailbox and get a tracking number (Promise). The response comes back later.

concepts.name: What is a Promise?
concepts.definition: A value that will be available in the future. States: pending (waiting), fulfilled (success), rejected (failed). JavaScript's way of handling operations that take time.
concepts.analogy: Ordering food at a restaurant. They give you a receipt (Promise) immediately. You can't eat the receipt, but it PROMISES you'll get food later.

concepts.name: .then() method
concepts.definition: Runs code AFTER a Promise resolves. You can chain multiple .then() calls. Each one returns a new Promise. fetch(url).then(res => res.json()).then(data => ...).
concepts.analogy: A to-do list with dependencies: "WHEN this finishes, THEN do this, THEN do this."

concepts.name: .catch() method
concepts.definition: Runs if ANY Promise in the chain fails. One .catch() at the end handles all errors. Essential for handling network failures and API errors.
concepts.analogy: A safety net at a circus — catches you no matter which trapeze you fall from.

concepts.name: Response object
concepts.definition: fetch() first returns a Response with metadata. response.ok is true if status is 200-299. Call response.json() to get the actual data (returns another Promise).
concepts.analogy: An envelope (response) with a letter inside (the data). You have to open it first (response.json()) to read what's inside.

understand.keyTakeaway: APIs return data as JSON. fetch() gets data but returns a Promise. Use .then() to handle the data when it arrives, and .catch() to handle errors.

build.goal: Extend the weather app to show multiple cities side-by-side for comparison
build.hint: Store all city data in an array, then use .map() to create HTML for each city card!

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn ASYNC/AWAIT — a cleaner, more readable way to write asynchronous code that looks like regular synchronous code.

accomplishments: Write asynchronous code that reads like normal code
Make your async code cleaner and easier to understand
hook.tagline: Stop writing .then().then().then(). Start writing code that looks synchronous but runs asynchronously.
learningObjectives: await pauses execution until Promise resolves
await only works inside async functions
Error handling is cleaner with async/await

do.title: Convert Promise Code to Async/Await
steps: Open console (F12) and click both buttons
Compare the code - see how much cleaner async/await is!
Notice they do the SAME thing, different syntax
do.code:
🔄 Promises vs Async/Await

😫 Old Way: .then() Chains

function getMovieOldWay() {
  fetch(apiUrl)
    .then(response => {
      return response.json();
    })
    .then(data => {
      console.log(data);
      displayMovie(data);
    })
    .catch(error => {
      console.error(error);
    });
}

😊 New Way: Async/Await

async function getMovieNewWay() {
  try {
    const response = await fetch(apiUrl);
    const data = await response.json();
    console.log(data);
    displayMovie(data);
  } catch (error) {
    console.error(error);
  }
}

📺 Console Output

Open browser console (F12) and click the buttons above!
do.result: Both approaches do the SAME thing, but async/await is much cleaner and easier to read!
do.proofRequired: showing movie data fetched using both methods

escapeHatches.text: async before a function makes it always return a Promise
await pauses the function until the Promise resolves

understand.title: How Async/Await Works

concepts.name: What is async?
concepts.definition: The `async` keyword before a function makes it automatically return a Promise.
concepts.rule: An async function ALWAYS returns a Promise, even if you return a regular value

concepts.name: What is await?
concepts.definition: The `await` keyword pauses the async function until the Promise resolves, then returns the resolved value.
concepts.important: await ONLY works inside async functions!

concepts.name: Error Handling with try/catch
concepts.definition: Use try/catch blocks to handle errors in async/await code.
concepts.benefit: try/catch is familiar to developers and works like other programming languages

patterns.use: When each request depends on the previous one
patterns.timing: Total = Sum of all request times

patterns.use: When requests are independent - MUCH faster!
When you want all results even if some fail
patterns.benefit: One failure doesn't stop the others

mistakes.fix: Always use await when calling async operations
Only use await inside async functions

understand.keyTakeaway: async/await makes asynchronous code look and behave like synchronous code. It's cleaner, more readable, and easier to debug than .then() chains.

escapeHatches.text: This app uses caching to avoid hitting the API too frequently - saves money and improves speed
Auto-refresh pauses when you switch tabs - smart performance optimization!

understand.title: Production-Ready Application Patterns

concepts.name: API Caching Strategy
concepts.definition: Store API responses temporarily to avoid redundant requests.
benefits: Reduces API costs (many APIs charge per request)
Faster user experience (instant loading from cache)

concepts.name: Auto-Refresh Pattern
concepts.definition: Automatically update data at regular intervals.
bestPractices: Pause when tab is hidden (saves battery/bandwidth)
Use exponential backoff if errors occur

concepts.name: Data Transformation
concepts.definition: Convert raw API data into formats suitable for display.

concepts.name: Search/Filter Implementation
concepts.definition: Client-side filtering of cached data for instant results.

concepts.name: Progressive Enhancement
concepts.definition: Build features that work even when parts fail.

levels.description: Basic data display - must always work
levels.fallback: Show cached data if API fails

levels.description: Search, filters, sorting - nice to have

techniques.description: Delay execution until user stops action

strategies.description: Show partial data when some requests fail
Show stale data if fresh data fails

understand.keyTakeaway: Production apps handle edge cases, optimize performance, cache intelligently, and degrade gracefully. Every feature should have a failure mode.

You've built:
